beats.text: Two models fine-tuned from one base are two points in the same weight space, and the line between them is often passable.
An ensemble runs both models and averages outputs, doubling inference cost; merging instead averages the weights once, offline.

visual.label: inference cost of an ensemble vs one merged model

beats.text: The plainest merge is a uniform mean of checkpoints that share a base; the result is what Wortsman calls a model soup.

visual.note: Uniform average sets each weight to 1/k

beats.text: Merging is element-wise, so it only makes sense when every ingredient shares the same base, architecture, and tokenizer.
Interpolating from a math model to a code model traces a family of compromise models as the mix coefficient runs from zero to one.
Whether that path stays low or crosses a barrier is the central empirical question for whether a merge works at all.
A task vector isolates exactly what a fine-tune changed: the displacement in weight space that learning the task produced.

visual.sub: Skills become vectors you can add and subtract

beats.text: Ilharco defines it as fine-tuned weights minus pretrained weights, with the base model serving as the origin of the coordinate system.

visual.note: Same shape as the weights, points toward the task

beats.text: Adding scaled task vectors installs skills, and adding several at once builds a multi-task model with no fine-tuning.

visual.note: One model that does both A and B

beats.text: Negation is the move practitioners reach for most: subtracting a toxicity vector reduces toxic generation while preserving fluency.
Building the multi-task merge can overshoot: with base 0.9 and task vectors 0.5 and 0.7, the merged coordinate reaches 2.1.

visual.label: merged coordinate from base 0.9 plus tau_A plus tau_B

beats.text: Task arithmetic is real but not magic: the scale alpha is a tuned dial, and adding vectors lets them collide coordinate by coordinate.
Summing task vectors works coordinate by coordinate, and that is exactly where merging many fine-tunes breaks down.

visual.sub: Why naive sums fail and how to fix them

beats.text: Yadav names two failure modes: redundant noise swamping the few meaningful values, and sign conflicts that cancel both tasks.
TIES trims to the top values, elects the sign holding more total magnitude, then averages only the agreeing tasks.

visual.note: Sign election by summed magnitude, not head-count

beats.text: DARE drops a fraction p of each vector's entries and rescales survivors, often discarding ninety percent with almost no loss.

visual.note: Inverted-dropout rescale keeps the vector unbiased

beats.text: For two models SLERP walks the arc on the hypersphere at constant angular speed, preserving norm instead of letting it sag.

visual.note: As the angle goes to zero SLERP reduces to the linear average

beats.text: With three trimmed values plus 0.2, plus 0.1, minus 0.6, the elected sign is negative because magnitude outvotes count.

concepts.title: The geometry that makes it work

beats.text: Averaging two neural networks is generally nonsense; merging is feasible only inside one specific geometric regime.

visual.sub: When the line between models stays low-loss

beats.text: Linear mode connectivity is the enabler: fine-tunes of one base sit in a single basin, so any convex combination is also low-loss.
The loss barrier measures how much the worst point on the path exceeds the average of the two endpoint losses.

visual.note: B near zero is safe; B much greater than zero is a barrier

beats.text: With midpoint loss 0.9 and endpoint losses 0.4 and 0.2, the barrier is 0.6, far too high to trust a naive average.

visual.label: loss barrier between two unconnected models

beats.text: Permutation symmetry means independently trained models sit in different copies of the basin, which is why the raw line crosses a barrier.
Ainsworth showed aligning permutations first can remove the barrier, so community fine-tunes of one base merge cleanly and from-scratch models do not.
Merging is nearly free: it needs no training data, no gradient steps, and no GPU beyond the memory to hold the checkpoints.

visual.sub: Cheap multi-skill models, with real trade-offs

beats.text: The dominant use is cheap multi-task models, combining separately trained specialists without ever assembling a joint training set.
The standard toolkit is mergekit, which implements soup, task arithmetic, TIES, DARE, SLERP, and Fisher behind one config file.
The limits are structural: same base, same architecture, finite capacity, and hyperparameters that demand a search rather than one setting.
A merge buys breadth sometimes at the cost of peak, so it rarely matches either specialist on its home benchmark.
A merge is a hypothesis, not a result: unproven until it clears the same evaluation suite you would demand of a fine-tune.

visual.label: evaluation you can skip after merging
